slides.title:
A Rosetta Stone
for your data
slides.subtitle: A semantic IR translates plain-English questions into SQL — the way the stone let scholars read a lost language through one they already knew.
slides.caption: The Rosetta Stone — photo by Hans Hillewaert, licensed CC BY-SA 4.0 (Wikimedia Commons).

slides.title: What is an IR (Intermediate Representation)?

rows.title: IR — a Semantic Query
rows.subtitle: a structured, validated pick: metrics · dimensions · filters · time

slides.caption: An IR is the structured middle step between a question and SQL — the same idea compilers use (source code → IR → machine code). The LLM writes the IR; deterministic code writes the SQL.

slides.title: Why not just let the LLM write SQL?

panels.title: LLM fills an IR
panels.subtitle: safe
lines: Only fields that exist in the model

slides.caption: The IR constrains the model to valid choices, then deterministic code does the rest — so it can't hallucinate columns, double-count, or drift.

slides.title: The pipeline: question → IR → SQL
slides.caption: The LLM never writes SQL — it only emits the IR. Everything from the IR down is pure, tested code, so it can't hallucinate columns or joins.

slides.title: The system prompt (built from the YAML model)
slides.code:
You translate natural-language questions into a structured query (an IR) for a semantic model. You do NOT write SQL.
Select only from the metrics and dimensions listed below; resolve the user's wording to these canonical names using the synonyms.
Always answer by calling the emit_query tool.

METRICS (aggregated measures):
- total_net_sales  [synonyms: sales, net sales, revenue, total sales, product sales]
- units_sold  [synonyms: units, quantity sold, items sold, volume]
- traffic  [synonyms: orders, order count, checks, transactions, traffic]
- total_budget  [synonyms: budget, budgeted sales, sales target, budget target, plan]

DIMENSIONS (group-by / filter attributes):
- product_name  [synonyms: item, product, sku name; examples: Cappuccino, Americano, Black Coffee]
- category_name  [synonyms: category, product category, item category; examples: Espresso Drinks, Coffee, Pastries]
- purchase_location  [synonyms: channel, sales channel, in-store vs online, where purchased; examples: At Shop, Online]
- date  [synonyms: business date, transaction date, day]
- iso_week  [synonyms: week, week number]
- iso_year  [synonyms: year]
- store_id  [synonyms: store, location, fc, store code, franchise code]
- market  [synonyms: market, area, territory; examples: Houston, Dallas, San Antonio]
- region  [synonyms: region, division]
- state  [synonyms: state]
- corporate_franchise  [synonyms: ownership type, corp vs franchise, corporate vs franchise; examples: Corporate, Franchise]
- lifecycle_stage  [synonyms: store status, lifecycle, operational status; examples: Open, Temporarily Closed, Terminated]

RULES:
- order_by fields must be among the selected metrics/dimensions.
- For 'last N days' style ranges use the time window (a date dimension + last_n_days).
- Filters compare a dimension to a value with one of: =, !=, <, <=, >, >=, in, not in, like.

EXAMPLES:
Q: How is Cappuccino performing week over week?
IR: {"metrics": ["total_net_sales", "units_sold"], "dimensions": ["product_name", "iso_year", "iso_week"], "filters": [{"field": "product_name", "op": "=", "value": "Cappuccino"}], "order_by": [{"field": "iso_week", "dir": "asc"}]}
Q: Net sales by market last 30 days
IR: {"metrics": ["total_net_sales"], "dimensions": ["market"], "time": {"field": "date", "last_n_days": 30}}
Q: Budget vs actual by store
IR: {"metrics": ["total_net_sales", "total_budget"], "dimensions": ["store_id"]}
slides.caption: Emitted by build_system_prompt(model). It is the LLM's rulebook — note line 1: 'You do NOT write SQL.' Sent as the system message; the user's question is the only user input.

slides.title: How the LLM translates a question into IR
slides.caption: Schema fixes the shape · prompt gives the words · examples show the pattern — so the IR only ever uses fields from your model.

slides.title: The IR pattern: the schema is a form, the planner fills it in
slides.intro: Q:  "How is Cappuccino performing week over week?"
slides.caption: The schema defines the blanks (and their types); the planner fills them in — same shape every time, so the compiler can trust it and the LLM can't drift into freeform SQL.

slides.title: The compiler: IR → SQL, deterministically
slides.caption: Same rules every time: each IR field maps to a fixed SQL clause. No LLM here — so the output is deterministic, parameterized (no injection), and SELECT-only.

slides.title: Anatomy of the product_sales semantic model
slides.caption: Six building blocks in one models/sales.yml: 3 tables joined by 2 relationships expose 4 facts, 12 dimensions and 4 metrics; 3 examples teach the planner. (Mirrors a Snowflake semantic view.)

slides.title: Synonyms: many phrasings → one canonical name
slides.caption: Every metric and dimension lists its synonyms, so the planner resolves free wording to one canonical name — the only name the schema, compiler and SQL ever see. This is what makes the NL → IR mapping work.
